elements.sent-2411-002: We propose a path-tracking Hybrid A* planner and a coupled hierarchical Model Predictive Control (MPC) controller in scenarios involving the path smoothing of agricultural vehicles.
elements.sent-2411-003: For agricultural vehicles following reference paths on farmlands, especially during cross-furrow operations, a minimum deviation from the reference path is desired, in addition to the curvature constraints and body scale collision avoidance.
elements.sent-2411-005: The path-tracking Hybrid A* can not only function in offline smoothing but also the real-time adjustment when confronted with unexpected obstacles.
elements.sent-2411-006: With the rapid advancements in robotics technologies in recent years, autonomous mobile robots have been extensively utilized across various domains, including industry, service, and agriculture\cite{moysiadis2020mobile .}
elements.sent-2411-007: Among all the techniques, smooth path planning is an indispensable component of any mobile robot.
elements.sent-2411-008: In the literature, smooth path planning studies are carried out in two steps, referred to as ``front end'' and ``back end''.
elements.sent-2411-009: The ``front end'' usually implements the sampling or search-based path planning algorithms, such as the A*\cite{zhang2024agv , Hybrid A*\cite{dolgov2008practical , rapidly-exploring random tree (RRT)\cite{tu2024improved , probabilistic roadmap (PRM)\cite{kavraki1998analysis and artificial potential field\cite{qin2024hierarchical , to generate a raw path from the start point to the end point while avoiding collision with obstacles in the environment.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-010: However, many path-planning algorithms, such as A*, RRT, and PRM, take no account of the robot's kinematic model, resulting in generating raw paths with sharp turns, which are difficult for the robot to track.
elements.sent-2411-011: The ``back end'', i.e., path smoothing, addresses this issue by modifying the raw path considering robot kinematic constraints, and obstacle avoidance, to generate spatial-temporal trajectories of the robot, which is easier for the robot to follow.
elements.sent-2411-012: However, these methods pay their most attention to geometric continuity and curvature limits, which are generic but not the most crucial elements in certain scenarios, such as the agricultural use introduced here.
elements.sent-2411-013: The cultivation between furrows requires the agricultural vehicle to cross the furrows.
elements.sent-2411-014: The reference trajectory for crossing the furrows is typically marked manually or extracted by ``front-end'' algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-015: During the cross-furrow operation, the vehicle should adhere closely to the reference paths to trample less on the farmlands and prevent crop damage, calling for the need to minimize the deviation degree of the smoothed trajectories.
elements.sent-2411-016: This has inspired us to propose a new methodology of path smoothing: using the search-based algorithm which contains a heuristic function and conforms to the nonholonomic constraints to directly solve the path smoothing problem.
elements.sent-2411-017: Therefore, a path-tracking Hybrid A* algorithm may be the most appropriate in this context.
elements.sent-2411-018: Path planning is always a key focus for agricultural robots \cite{santos2020path with unique challenges, such as terrain irregularity, multi-task handling, field coverage, etc. Santos et al.\cite{santos2020path1 limit the roll, pitch, and yaw angles considering the center of mass of the robot to tackle the irregularity of farmland terrain, and Mai et al.\cite{mai2019path find a solution for multi-point monitoring process in potato cultivation.}}}
elements.sent-2411-019: Besides, field coverage\cite{hameed2014intelligent,rahman2019optimum,tan2021comprehensive and energy optimization\cite{yan2020agrirover are also discussed.}}
elements.sent-2411-020: However, research on avoiding crop damage is relatively rare, which inspires us to design algorithms that minimize the deviation from the reference trajectory.
elements.sent-2411-021: In recent years, there have been many works \cite{sedighi2019guided, sheng2021autonomous, zheng2023navigation, chang2024hybrid, meng2023improved focusing on Hybrid A* and its application.}
elements.sent-2411-022: Hybrid A* is a search-based path planning algorithm that combines the discrete grid search of A* with continuous space optimization, enabling the generation of feasible and smooth paths that account for vehicle kinematics.
elements.sent-2411-023: Recent works apply the Hybrid A* algorithm in conjunction with other methods: with the Visibility Diagram planning\cite{sedighi2019guided , with the safe travel corridor model\cite{sheng2021autonomous , with the time-elastic-band\cite{zheng2023navigation , with hierarchical clustering\cite{chang2024hybrid , and with multistage dynamic optimization\cite{meng2023improved , to enhance computational speed and optimize local trajectories.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-024: However, to the best of our knowledge, all these works utilize the Hybrid A* path planner as the ``front end'' in navigation or parking scenarios, while no previous work has applied it in path smoothing.
elements.sent-2411-025: The state-of-the-art path smoothing methods include the following types\cite{ravankar2018path : interpolation, special curves, and optimization-based path smoothing.}
elements.sent-2411-026: First, interpolation methods involve employing parameterized curves, such as the polynomial curve, the Bézier curve, or the B-spline curve, to fit the raw path generated by the front end.
elements.sent-2411-027: The polynomial interpolation is a traditional method and is limited by Runge’s phenomenon\cite{epperson1987runge .}
elements.sent-2411-028: The Bézier curve interpolation is researched thoroughly due to its capability to satisfy $C^2$ continuity and curvature constraints\cite{yang2010analytical , or endpoints and via points constraints\cite{kawabata2015path .}}
elements.sent-2411-029: Low-order Bézier curves such as quadratic Bézier curves are used to prune the control points\cite{durakli2022new or even build curvature-continuous smooth path\cite{liu2024high .}}
elements.sent-2411-030: In terms of B-spline, real-time $C^2$ continuous B-splines with curvature limits\cite{elbanhawi2015continuous or with collision avoidance\cite{noreen2020collision are widely used in the path smoothing literature, and recent B-spline path smoothing method \cite{li2022smooth further eliminate jerky and unnecessary motions by introducing the Douglas-Peucker algorithm into the path pruning technique.}}}
elements.sent-2411-031: Second, path smoothing based on special curves mainly includes clothoid and Dubin's curve, which are difficult to parameterize compared to the interpolation curves.
elements.sent-2411-032: The clothoids, also known as Euler spirals\cite{havemann2013curvature , are parameterized by their arc length and show a linear variation in curvature along their length.}
elements.sent-2411-033: Lambert et al.\cite{lambert2021optimal exhibit that clothoid-based path smoothing can be used to automatically plan diversions that can be traversed at high speed by automated vehicles, and Lin et al.\cite{lin2024clothoid use clothoid to generate smooth emergency-stopping paths.}}
elements.sent-2411-034: Dubin’s curve\cite{dubins1957curves and Reeds-Shepp curve\cite{reeds1990optimal , which consist of line segments and circular arcs, are used to find the shortest paths between two configurations conforming to vehicle kinematic constraints\cite{park2022three .}}}
elements.sent-2411-035: The last category, the optimization-based method, is mostly seen combined with the methods mentioned above in the literature, which can simultaneously consider multiple optimization objectives, such as obstacle avoidance, rollover constraints, speed, acceleration, jerk, etc, to find a balanced solution.
elements.sent-2411-036: The current mainstream optimization methods can be adapted to the curves mentioned above, such as the particle swarm optimization (PSO) to the Bézier curve\cite{xu2017global,song2021improved,xu2022new , and the ant colony optimization (ACO) to the B-spline curve\cite{feng2022path,huo2024new .}}
elements.sent-2411-037: However, the abovementioned methods have two major drawbacks when applied to our circumstances.
elements.sent-2411-038: Most importantly, most works pay no attention to minimize the deviation degree between the smoothed trajectory and the reference path, which is a key requirement in our agricultural scenario.
elements.sent-2411-039: In addition, most works generate trajectories that avoid collision based on the point-mass model, neglecting the vehicle's size, while in our context the distance from the reference trajectory to the boundary is at the same scale as the vehicle size, which makes whole-body collision avoidance necessary.
elements.sent-2411-040: To bridge the gaps, we propose a novel cross-furrow path planning method for agricultural vehicles.
elements.sent-2411-041: The main contribution of this paper is threefold: First, we propose the path-tracking Hybrid A*, which satisfies nonholonomic constraints and vehicle size collision avoidance, bring it into the field of path smoothing, and devise new cost and heuristic functions to minimize the deviation degree.
elements.sent-2411-042: Note that the path-tracking Hybrid A* can be used in not only the offline smoothing of the reference paths but also the real-time adjustments of the smoothed trajectories when confronted with obstacles unknown beforehand.
elements.sent-2411-043: Second, we adopt a hierarchical MPC to track the smoothed trajectory, with the initial solution solved by linearized MPC and nonlinear local adjustments around the initial solution.
elements.sent-2411-044: Third, we carry out extensive simulations with baseline comparisons based on real-world farm datasets to evaluate the performance of our algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-045: The remainder of this paper is organized as follows: \Cref{formulation mathematically defines the model of farm and agricultural vehicles.}
elements.sent-2411-046: We are considering the cross-furrow path planning problem for agricultural vehicles when operating on farmland.
elements.sent-2411-047: The agricultural vehicles are tasked with planning their motion trajectory, closely following the reference path between different furrows, which is manually annotated or generated by the front-end algorithm, while ensuring avoidance constraints with the edges of the field.
elements.sent-2411-048: We formulate the farm as a two-dimensional polygon-shaped region $P\in\mathbb{R ^2$, which is defined by the polygon vertices $\mathcal{V = \{v_1, v_2, \dots, v_n\}$ and edges $\mathcal{E = \{e_1, e_2, \dots, e_n\}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-049: Within the farm $P$, the movement of agricultural vehicles should align with the cross-furrow reference paths.
elements.sent-2411-050: Each reference path $R\subset P$ is a polyline consisting of a sequence of line segments $\mathcal{L = \{l_1, l_2, \dots, l_m\}$, where each line segment $l_j\subset P$ connects waypoints $p_j$ and $p_{j+1}$.}
elements.sent-2411-051: We consider an agricultural vehicle formulated as a Wheeled Mobile Robot (WMR).
elements.sent-2411-052: Specifically, the kinematics of the WMR is formulated as the following bicycle model: \begin{equation}\begin{cases} \dot{x} = v \cos\theta, \dot{y} = v \sin\theta, \dot{\theta} = v\frac{tan\delta}{L}, \dot{v} = a, \end{cases} \label{eq:case1}\end{equation} where $L$ denotes the wheelbase, and the state vector $\boldsymbol{x = [x, y, \theta, v]^T$ indicates the configuration of the WMR, including the position $[x,y]^T\in\mathbb{R ^2$, orientation $\theta\in\mathbb{R}$, and linear velocity of the rear wheel axis center, $C$.}}
elements.sent-2411-053: The control input $\boldsymbol{u = [\delta, a]^T$ comprises the wheel steering angle $\delta$ (with a maximum value $\delta_m$) and the linear acceleration $a$.}
elements.sent-2411-054: Applying Euler's approximation to \cref{eq:case1 , we obtain the discrete-time version of the kinematic model: \begin{equation}\begin{cases} x_{k+1} = x_k+v_k \cos\theta_k\Delta t, y_{k+1} = y_k+v_k \sin\theta_k\Delta t, \theta_{k+1} = \theta_k+v_k\frac{tan\delta_k}{L}\Delta t, v_{k+1} = v_k+a_k\Delta t, \end{cases} \label{eq:case2}\end{equation} where $\Delta t$ is the sampling interval.}
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation} x_{k+1} = x_k+v_k \cos\theta_k\Delta t, y_{k+1} = y_k+v_k \sin\theta_k\Delta t, \theta_{k+1} = \theta_k+v_k\frac{tan\delta_k}{L}\Delta t, v_{k+1} = v_k+a_k\Delta t,  \label{eq:case2}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-055: For notational simplicity, we reformulate \cref{eq:case2 as the following compact representation: \begin{equation}\boldsymbol{x}_{k+1} = f_d(\boldsymbol{x}_k, \boldsymbol{u}_k). \label{eq:model2}\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-056: Our objective is to find a smoothed trajectory $\tau(t): \mathbb{R \to \mathbb{R ^2$ for the agricultural vehicle, which is confined in $\mathcal{V}$ and conforms to the kinematics (\cref{eq:case1 ), and has a minimum deviation degree from $\mathcal{R}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-057: The key points of the smoothed trajectory are defined as $x_n = \tau(n\Delta t), n = 0, 1, 2, \dots$, where $\Delta t$ is the sampling interval.
elements.sent-2411-058: Our approach is divided into two stages.
elements.sent-2411-059: Initially, by modifying the Hybrid A* algorithm, we devise novel heuristic functions to generate smoothed trajectories that are closely aligned with the reference paths, which is shown in \cref{alg:1 .}
elements.sent-2411-060: Besides, \cref{real-time-theory shows the adjusted path-tracking Hybrid A* method of real-time obstacle avoidance.}
elements.sent-2411-061: Second, \cref{MPC incorporates the hierarchical MPC algorithm, which robustly tracks the smoothed trajectory.}
elements.sent-2411-062: The Hybrid A* algorithm \cite{dolgov2008practical is an extension of the traditional A* path-search algorithm, which is designed to handle continuous spaces and nonholonomic constraints (constraints on the motion of vehicles that cannot be captured by purely holonomic constraints) for path planning.}
elements.sent-2411-063: Here we briefly review the basic principles of Hybrid A*.. Search Space.
elements.sent-2411-064: Hybrid A* generates paths through a blend of discrete and continuous space exploration.
elements.sent-2411-065: Specifically, Hybrid A* discretizes the continuous search space into a grid, while allowing movements based on the kinematic model of the vehicle instead of being limited to grid alignments as traditional A* does.
elements.sent-2411-066: This approach helps Hybrid A* to closely represent vehicle dynamics and constraints, such as turning radii, in the process of finding the optimal path.
elements.sent-2411-067: The search process is based on nodes, each containing the information of current state $[x,y,\theta]^T$, the previous node, and the total cost $f$.
elements.sent-2411-068: Note that each grid can only contain one forward and backward node respectively.
elements.sent-2411-070: Hybrid A* employs both a cost function $g$ and a heuristic function $h$ to optimize pathfinding.
elements.sent-2411-071: The cost $g$ accounts for the accumulated distance from the start, adding penalties for vehicular maneuvers such as steering changes or reversing.
elements.sent-2411-072: The heuristic function $h$, which is the max of the non-holonomic-without-obstacles cost and 2D Euclidean distance \cite{dolgov2008practical , determines the direction of the search, preventing aimless exploration and thus improving search speed.}
elements.sent-2411-073: In general, the total cost function to be minimized can be stated as \begin{equation}f = g + h. \label{eq:h1}\end{equation}.

elements.subsection-2411-005: Deviation-Aware Cost and Heuristic for Path Smoothing
elements.sent-2411-074: One of the most significant contributions of our work is the design of deviation-aware cost and heuristic functions to adapt the Hybrid A* for path smoothing.
elements.sent-2411-075: As stated in \cref{A* principle , in traditional Hybrid A*, the cost and heuristic functions were mainly aimed at minimizing the length of the planned trajectory.}
elements.sent-2411-076: However, in our problem context, it is imperative to also take into account the degree of deviation from a reference path, thus guiding the generated trajectory to more closely align with the reference path.
elements.sent-2411-077: First, we defined the distance from a node to the polyline reference path: \begin{equation}dis(x,R) = \min_{i=1,2...m} d(x,l_i), \label{eq:dis}\end{equation} where $d(x,l_i)$ denotes the distance from the point $x$ to the line segment $l_i$.
elements.sent-2411-078: Based on \cref{eq:dis , we propose a cost function $e_{cost (x)$ to evaluate the current deviation degree and a heuristic function $e_{pred (x)$ to estimate the future deviation degree.}}}
elements.sent-2411-079: For the former function, we proposed a new metric of the deviation degree between the robot's generated trajectory and the reference path $R$: \begin{equation}e_{cost}(x) = \int_{s(x_s)}^{s(x)} dis(x(s),R)ds, \label{eq:int1}\end{equation} where we define $x(s): \mathbb{R \to \mathbb{R ^2$ as the expression of $\tau(t)$ parameterized by arc length $s$, with $s(x)$ representing the inverse mapping of $x(s)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-080: The point $x_s$ denotes the start point of the generated trajectory.
elements.sent-2411-081: This metric takes into account the cumulative deviation along the trajectory length, and can approximately be regarded as the area enclosed by the generated trajectory and the reference path, which is shown in \cref{fig:1 (a).}
elements.sent-2411-082: For computational convenience, we discretized the integration expression given in \cref{eq:int1 and subsequently derived the cost function $e_{cost (x)$ for computing the current cumulative degree of deviation: \begin{equation}e_{cost}(x_n) \simeq \sum_{i=1}^{n} dis(x_i,R)\cdot \Delta s_i, \label{eq:ecost}\end{equation} where $\Delta s_i$ is the distance from $x_{i-1}$ to $x_i$, and $x_i$ denotes the key points comprising the generated trajectory.}}
elements.sent-2411-083: Analogous to \cref{eq:int1 , we aim to compute the heuristic $e_{pred (x)$ as the deviation between the predicted trajectory and the reference path: \begin{equation}e_{pred}(x) = \int_{s(x)}^{s(x_{g})} dis(x(s),R)ds, \label{eq:int2}\end{equation} where $x_g$ denotes the goal point of the reference path.}}
elements.sent-2411-084: Different from the integral \cref{eq:int1 above, \cref{eq:int2 requires integrating the deviation along the robot's predictive trajectory that remains unknown.}}
elements.sent-2411-085: To tackle this issue, we propose to use a Reed-and-Shepp path, which connects $(x, \theta)$ and $(x_g, \theta_g)$ by straight lines and circular arcs, as the integral path, where $\theta$ and $\theta_g$ respectively denotes the orientation at $x$ and $x_g$.
elements.sent-2411-086: If the orientation of the current node significantly diverges from the reference path, the accumulated deviation distance along the Reed-and-Shepp path will be substantial, leading to a significantly large $e_{pred}$, which is illustrated in \cref{fig:1 (b).}
elements.sent-2411-087: By discretizing the integration, the expression is formulated as follows: \begin{equation}e_{pred}(x_n) \simeq \sum_{j=1}^{N}dis(x_j,R) \cdot \Delta s_j, \label{eq:epred}\end{equation} where $x_j (j=1,2,3...,N)$ denotes the sample points on the Reed-and-Shepp path, and $\Delta s_j$ denotes the length of Reed-and-Shepp path between $x_{j-1}$ and $x_j$.
elements.sent-2411-088: By incorporating the new cost and heuristic functions \cref{eq:ecost,eq:epred into \cref{eq:h1 , the total cost to minimize for a node is \begin{equation}f = g+h+e_{cost}+e_{pred}. \label{eq:totalcost}\end{equation}.}}
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Path Tracking Hybrid A* Algorithm} \label{alg:1} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \State Data: Start node $s$, target node $t$, \Statex reference path $R$, map $M$ \State Result: Path from $s$ to $t$ \State Initialize an empty priority queue $O$ and an empty set $C$\label{initialize} \State $O.\operatorname{insert}(s)$ \label{insert1} \State $p_{old} \gets \text{Null}$ \While{$O$ is not empty} \State $p \gets O.\operatorname{pop}()$ \label{pop} \State $C.\operatorname{insert}(p)$ \If{$p$ == $t$} \label{success} \State \Return the path from $s$ to $p$ \EndIf \If{$\operatorname{dist}(p, t) < d_0$} \label{close} \If{$r=\operatorname{RS}(p,t)$ is valid} \label{RS} \State $f(p,t)=g(p)+e_{cost}(p)+g(r)+e_{cost}(r)$ \label{ft} \If{$p_{old} \neq \text{Null} and f(p, t)>f(p_{old}, t) $} \label{prune11} \State Continue \Comment{Pruning 1} \EndIf \label{prune12} \State $O.\operatorname{insert}(t)$ \label{insert_t} \State $p_{old} \gets p$ \EndIf \ElsIf{$p_{old} \neq \text{Null}$} \label{prune21} \State Continue \Comment{Pruning 2} \EndIf \label{prune22} \State $O.\operatorname{insert}(\operatorname{ValidChildren}(p, M))$ \label{insert2} \EndWhile \If{$O$ is empty} \label{fail} \State \Return path not found \EndIf \end{algorithmic}

elements.subsection-2411-007: Path Tracking Hybrid A* for Real-time Obstacle Avoidance
elements.sent-2411-114: Although the smoothed trajectories can be generated in advance by methods in \cref{path tracking , unexpected obstacles, such as other agricultural vehicles parked on the farmland, could occur.}
elements.sent-2411-115: Therefore, real-time and safe replanning of the smoothed trajectory plays a vital role in the practical execution of the planning-control algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-116: The real-time obstacle avoidance problem is formulated as follows.
elements.sent-2411-117: The set of obstacles is defined as $\mathcal{O _b = \{O_b | O_b \subset P\}$, in which $O_b$ is a convex polygon defined as $O_b = \{o_1, o_2, \dots, o_{n_b}\}$, where $o_i \in \mathbb{R ^2, i=1,2,\dots,n_b$ are the vertices and $n_b$ is the number of vertices.}}
elements.sent-2411-118: Note that the obstacles are not known before they are detected by the vehicle during driving.
elements.sent-2411-119: The vehicle has a limited detection range of $R_f$ and a limited field of view (FOV) $\alpha_f$, which forms a fan-shaped area $F_f$.
elements.sent-2411-120: When all the vertices of $O_b$ have been in the area of $F_f$, the obstacle is detected by the vehicle.
elements.sent-2411-121: In this circumstance, we need to replan the trajectory to ensure the collision-free driving of the vehicle.
elements.sent-2411-122: The working principle of the path-tracking Hybrid A*, when the vehicle discovers random obstacles, is as follows: First, the vehicle extracts the start and end states that collide with the obstacles on the original smoothed trajectory, and then the range of the original trajectory to be replanned are taken a few points wider.
elements.sent-2411-123: Then, this range of the original smoothed trajectory is used as the reference trajectory for the real-time replanning of path-tracking Hybrid A*, which returns a trajectory with vehicle body scale collision avoidance.
elements.sent-2411-124: Finally, the replanned trajectory replaces the corresponding part of the origin trajectory, which is to be tracked using our hierarchical MPC, as will be introduced in \cref{MPC .}

elements.sent-2411-204: We propose a path-tracking Hybrid A* planner and a coupled hierarchical MPC controller for the path smoothing of agricultural vehicles.
elements.sent-2411-205: We devise new cost and heuristic functions to minimize the deviation degree.
elements.sent-2411-206: The proposed planning-control system results in smooth and obstacle-free trajectories for agricultural vehicles, both in offline and real-time scenarios, as shown in the experiments on real-world farm datasets.
elements.sent-2411-207: Future directions include either the whole field path planning, to achieve maximum field coverage in addition to the minimum deviation, or the multi-vehicle collaboration, to allocate the tasks of agricultural operations to each vehicle with the minimum total deviation.. \bibliographystyle{ieeetr.}
elements.sent-2411-208: We propose a Path-Tracking Hybrid A* planner coupled with a hierarchical Model Predictive Control (MPC) framework for path smoothing in agricultural vehicles.
elements.sent-2411-209: The goal is to minimize deviation from reference paths during cross-furrow operations, thereby optimizing operational efficiency, preventing crop and soil damage, while also enforcing curvature constraints and ensuring full-body collision avoidance.
elements.sent-2411-210: Our contributions are threefold: (1) We develop the Path-Tracking Hybrid A* algorithm to generate smooth trajectories that closely adhere to the reference trajectory, respect strict curvature constraints, and satisfy full-body collision avoidance.
elements.sent-2411-211: The adherence is achieved by designing novel cost and heuristic functions to minimize tracking errors under nonholonomic constraints.
elements.sent-2411-212: By using linearized MPC to warm-start the nonlinear solver, the framework improves the convergence of nonlinear optimization with minimal loss in accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-213: Simulations on real-world farm datasets demonstrate superior performance compared to baseline methods in safety, path adherence, computation speed, and real-time obstacle avoidance.
elements.sent-2411-214: With the rapid advancement of robotics technologies \cite{wang2022applications , \cite{cheng2023recent , autonomous mobile robots have demonstrated great potential in various agricultural applications, including crop monitoring \cite{bayati2018mobile , precision spraying \cite{meshram2022pesticide , harvesting \cite{fue2020extensive , and autonomous weeding \cite{bawden2017robot .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-215: These robots enable high-efficiency operations in large-scale farmlands, significantly reducing human labor and improving productivity.
elements.sent-2411-216: In this context, path planning emerges as a fundamental capability for agricultural robots.
elements.sent-2411-217: Prior studies have explored diverse challenges, such as terrain irregularity—addressed through attitude stabilization and center-of-mass constraints \cite{santos2020path1 , multi-task execution—such as simultaneous monitoring and navigation during cultivation \cite{mai2019path , and field coverage—optimized via systematic sweeping and coverage strategies \cite{wang2025hybrid , \cite{lei2022deep , \cite{din2022deep .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-218: Additional works also consider energy-efficient routing \cite{yan2020agrirover , among other practical issues.}
elements.sent-2411-219: An important yet insufficiently studied task in agricultural robotics is path planning for cross-furrow operations, where vehicles must traverse furrows while closely following a predefined reference trajectory, typically obtained through manual annotation or front-end planning algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-220: The primary objective in this scenario is to minimize deviation from the reference path, as even small lateral errors may lead to crop damage, soil compaction, or inefficiencies in field operations.
elements.sent-2411-221: Path smoothing techniques—which refine raw paths into smooth and kinematically feasible trajectories—have shown promise in addressing complex navigation tasks.
elements.sent-2411-222: For example, interpolation-based methods using Bézier or B-spline curves \cite{yang2010analytical, noreen2020collision , special-curve approaches such as clothoids and Dubins paths \cite{lambert2021optimal, reeds1990optimal , and optimization-based methods \cite{song2021improved, feng2022path have been widely applied in general-purpose robotics.}}}
elements.sent-2411-223: However, these methods face significant limitations in cross-furrow agricultural scenarios: First, they often prioritize geometric smoothness or curvature constraints, but rarely aim to minimize deviation from a reference trajectory, which is crucial to prevent crop damage.
elements.sent-2411-224: Second, most approaches assume point-mass models, overlooking the vehicle’s full dimensions—making them unsuitable in narrow farmland settings where whole-body collision avoidance is required.
elements.sent-2411-225: Therefore, generating kinematically feasible trajectories that closely follow reference paths in constrained farmland remains an open challenge.
elements.sent-2411-226: To bridge this gap, we propose a novel cross-furrow path planning and control framework for agricultural vehicles with three key contributions.
elements.sent-2411-227: First, we develop the Path-Tracking Hybrid A* algorithm to generate smooth trajectories that adhere closely to the reference trajectory while respecting strict curvature constraints and satisfying full-body collision avoidance.
elements.sent-2411-228: Second, we propose an online replanning strategy as an extension of Path-Tracking Hybrid A* that enables real-time avoidance of unforeseen obstacles, while leveraging pruning techniques to enhance computational efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-229: Third, we design a hierarchical Model Predictive Control (MPC) framework that ensures tight path adherence and real-time satisfaction of vehicle constraints, including nonholonomic dynamics and full-body collision avoidance.
elements.sent-2411-230: By using linearized MPC to warm-start the nonlinear solver, the framework improves convergence of the nonlinear optimization with a minor loss in accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-231: Simulations on real-world farm datasets demonstrate superior performance over baselines in safety, adherence to reference paths, computation speed, and real-time response to unforeseen obstacles.
elements.sent-2411-232: The structure of this paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-233: In \Cref{formulation , the model for the farm and agricultural vehicles is formulated.}
elements.sent-2411-234: Finally, \Cref{simulation provides comparative simulation results against baseline methods, covering both static path tracking and real-time obstacle avoidance scenarios.}
elements.sent-2411-235: In path planning, the process is typically divided into a front-end and a back-end.
elements.sent-2411-236: The front-end focuses on generating a raw path from the start to the goal while avoiding obstacles, often using sampling- or search-based algorithms such as Hybrid A* \cite{dolgov2008practical .}
elements.sent-2411-237: The back-end, or path smoothing, refines this raw path by considering the vehicle's kinematic constraints and other factors, ensuring the trajectory is feasible and smooth for the robot to follow.
elements.sent-2411-238: In recent years, a substantial body of research has explored the Hybrid A* algorithm and its variants for path planning applications \cite{sedighi2019guided, sheng2021autonomous, zheng2023navigation, chang2024hybrid, meng2023improved .}
elements.sent-2411-239: Hybrid A* integrates the discrete grid-based search of A* with continuous-space refinement, enabling the generation of kinematically feasible and smooth trajectories.
elements.sent-2411-240: Recent studies have enhanced the algorithm's performance by incorporating it with various techniques, such as visibility diagrams \cite{sedighi2019guided , safe travel corridor models \cite{sheng2021autonomous , time-elastic bands \cite{zheng2023navigation , hierarchical clustering \cite{chang2024hybrid , and multistage dynamic optimization frameworks \cite{meng2023improved .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-241: These efforts primarily aim to improve computational efficiency and local trajectory quality.
elements.sent-2411-242: However, to the best of our knowledge, Hybrid A* has been predominantly employed as a front-end planner in navigation or parking contexts, and its potential for path smoothing has yet to be explored.
elements.sent-2411-243: State-of-the-art path smoothing approaches can be broadly classified into three categories: interpolation-based, special-curve-based, and optimization-based methods \cite{ravankar2018path .}
elements.sent-2411-244: Interpolation-based methods employ parameterized curves—such as polynomial, Bézier, and B-spline curves—to fit coarse paths generated by front-end planners.
elements.sent-2411-245: While polynomial interpolation is classical, it suffers from limitations like Runge’s phenomenon \cite{epperson1987runge .}
elements.sent-2411-246: Bézier curves are well-studied for enforcing $C^2$ continuity and curvature constraints \cite{yang2010analytical .}
elements.sent-2411-247: Recent advances use low-order Bézier curves (e.g., quadratic) to reduce control point complexity \cite{durakli2022new and enable curvature-continuous path generation \cite{liu2024high .}}
elements.sent-2411-248: Similarly, B-splines support real-time generation with smoothness and curvature constraints \cite{elbanhawi2015continuous , and are often integrated with collision avoidance modules \cite{noreen2020collision .}}
elements.sent-2411-249: Special-curve-based methods utilize geometric primitives such as clothoids and Dubins curves, which are more difficult to parameterize than interpolation curves but naturally reflect vehicle kinematics.
elements.sent-2411-250: Clothoids, or Euler spirals, feature linearly varying curvature and have been applied in high-speed autonomous navigation \cite{lambert2021optimal and emergency stopping path design \cite{lin2024clothoid .}}
elements.sent-2411-251: Dubins and Reeds-Shepp curves \cite{dubins1957curves, reeds1990optimal consist of circular arcs and straight lines, and are commonly used for generating shortest feasible paths under nonholonomic constraints \cite{park2022three .}}
elements.sent-2411-252: Optimization-based methods typically augment the above techniques by formulating the smoothing task as a multi-objective optimization problem.
elements.sent-2411-253: These methods can jointly consider collision avoidance, dynamic constraints (e.g., speed, acceleration, jerk), and environmental factors.
elements.sent-2411-254: Metaheuristic algorithms such as particle swarm optimization (PSO) have been applied to Bézier-based smoothing \cite{ song2021improved, xu2022new , while ant colony optimization (ACO) has been used in B-spline smoothing frameworks \cite{feng2022path, huo2024new .}}
elements.sent-2411-255: However, these methods often prioritize geometric smoothness or curvature constraints, while neglecting to minimize deviation from a reference trajectory—an essential factor for crop protection.
elements.sent-2411-256: Additionally, their reliance on point-mass models fails to account for the vehicle’s full dimensions, limiting their applicability in constrained farmland environments where whole-body collision avoidance is critical.
elements.sent-2411-257: The work on path planning for agricultural robots can be mainly divided into three areas: point-to-point planning, coverage planning, and multi-robot collaborative path planning, which will be introduced separately in the following.
elements.sent-2411-258: In Point-to-Point path planning, Voronoi diagrams \cite{habib2016mobile and variants of Ant Colony Optimization (ACO) \cite{li2022path are employed to generate safe and optimal paths from the start to the end point.}}
elements.sent-2411-259: Additionally, learning-based methods are also utilized; for instance, \cite{zhao2024improved employs a 3D neural network to enable collision-free navigation for robots with arbitrary shapes, while \cite{yang2022intelligent applies deep reinforcement learning to facilitate dynamic obstacle avoidance.}}
elements.sent-2411-260: For complete coverage path planning (CCPP), the most commonly used basic methods for simple path planning are the nested and reciprocating approaches \cite{ning2022research .}
elements.sent-2411-261: Hybrid variants, which combine these basic methods with other techniques, demonstrate improved performance.
elements.sent-2411-262: Notable examples include the use of fully convolutional neural networks (FCNN) for workspace edge detection and optimized path generation \cite{lei2022deep , inner spiral methods to reduce non-working path length \cite{wang2025hybrid , and a CNN-based Dual Deep Q-learning (DDQN) algorithm that adapts to dynamic agricultural environments \cite{din2022deep .}}}
elements.sent-2411-263: In the field of multi-robot collaborative path planning, several studies focus on simultaneous task and path planning to enhance coverage efficiency, employing methods such as the improved Dijkstra algorithm \cite{wang2023collaborative and particle swarm optimization \cite{liu2021optimization .}}
elements.sent-2411-264: Additionally, path conflicts between different robots are a critical concern, which is addressed in \cite{cao2023global using topographic maps and time windows.}
elements.sent-2411-265: However, the aforementioned methods neither explicitly consider the adherence to reference trajectory tracking nor incorporate full-body collision avoidance within the planning framework.
elements.sent-2411-266: The objective is to generate motion trajectories that closely follow a given reference path—either manually annotated or generated by a front-end algorithm—while satisfying both nonholonomic constraints and collision-avoidance constraints with respect to the field boundaries.
elements.sent-2411-267: The farmland is formulated as a two-dimensional polygonal region as a two-dimensional polygon-shaped region $P\subset\mathbb{R ^2$, which is defined by the polygon vertices $\mathcal{V = \{\boldsymbol{v _1, \boldsymbol{v _2, \dots, \boldsymbol{v _n\}$ and edges $\mathcal{E = \{e_1, e_2, \dots, e_n\}$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-268: Each reference path \( R \subset P \) is represented as a polyline, defined by an ordered sequence of points \( \{\boldsymbol{r _0, \boldsymbol{r _1, \dots, \boldsymbol{r _{m}\} \subset P \).}}}
elements.sent-2411-269: The polyline consists of line segments \( \mathcal{L = \{l_1, l_2, \dots, l_m\} \), where each segment \( l_j \) connects \( r_{j-1 \) and \( r_{j \).}}}

elements.sent-2411-272: The agricultural vehicle is formulated as a Wheeled Mobile Robot (WMR).
elements.sent-2411-273: Specifically, the kinematics of the WMR is formulated as the following bicycle model: \begin{equation}\begin{cases} \dot{x} = v \cos\theta, \dot{y} = v \sin\theta, \dot{\theta} = v\frac{tan\delta}{L}, \dot{v} = a, \end{cases} \label{eq:case1}\end{equation} where $L$ denotes the wheelbase, and the state vector $\boldsymbol{x = [x, y, \theta, v]^T$ indicates the configuration of the WMR, including the position $[x,y]^T\in\mathbb{R ^2$, orientation $\theta\in[0,2\pi]$, and linear velocity $v$ of the rear wheel axis center $C$.}}

elements.sent-2411-275: For notational simplicity, we reformulate \cref{eq:case2 as the following compact representation: \begin{equation}\boldsymbol{x}_{k+1} = f_d(\boldsymbol{x}_k, \boldsymbol{u}_k). \label{eq:model2}\end{equation} Our objective is twofold: (1) Trajectory smoothing: to generate a smoothed trajectory represented as a sequence of key states \( \{\boldsymbol{x _{r,n}\}_{n=0 ^T\), where each state \( \boldsymbol{x _{r,n =[\boldsymbol{z _n,\theta_n,v_n] \) corresponds to the vehicle’s state at time \( t_n = n\Delta t \), $\boldsymbol{z _n=[x_n,y_n]$, and $T$ is the maximum time step.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-276: Note that the smoothed trajectory should start from the $\boldsymbol{r _0$ and reach $\boldsymbol{r _m$, with the orientation at both the start and the end consistent with that of the reference path, i.e. $\theta_0=\phi_1,\theta_T=\phi_m$, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-277: The planned trajectory must satisfy the vehicle’s kinematic constraints (see \cref{eq:case2 ), avoid full-body collisions with the farmland boundaries, and minimize the deviation from the reference path \( R \).}
elements.sent-2411-278: The controller should aim to minimize tracking errors while ensuring control effort remains within physical limits.
elements.sent-2411-279: Our approach consists of two sequential stages.
elements.sent-2411-280: In the first stage, the Path-Tracking Hybrid A\* algorithm is proposed to generate a smoothed trajectory \( \{\boldsymbol{x _{r,n}\}_{n=0 ^T \).}}
elements.sent-2411-281: To improve adherence to the reference path, novel cost and heuristic functions are introduced, and pruning strategies are incorporated to enhance computational efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-282: In addition, a real-time obstacle avoidance extension of the Path-Tracking Hybrid A\* algorithm is developed.
elements.sent-2411-283: In the second stage, the hierarchical MPC is employed to track the planned trajectory with high accuracy and safety.
elements.sent-2411-284: Specifically, a linearized MPC is used to warm-start the nonlinear solver, thereby accelerating convergence with a minor compromise in accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-285: The Path-Tracking Hybrid A* generates trajectories through a blend of discrete and continuous space exploration.
elements.sent-2411-286: Specifically, the Path-Tracking Hybrid A* discretizes the continuous search space into grids, while allowing movements based on the kinematic model of the vehicle instead of being limited to grid alignments.
elements.sent-2411-287: This approach helps the Path-Tracking Hybrid A* to closely represent vehicle dynamics and constraints, such as turning radii, in the process of finding the optimal path.
elements.sent-2411-288: The Path-Tracking Hybrid A* utilizes motion primitives to extend the trajectory.

elements.sent-2411-424: We propose a novel cross-furrow path planning and control framework for agricultural vehicles, integrating a path-tracking Hybrid A* planner with a coupled hierarchical MPC controller.
elements.sent-2411-425: To minimize deviation from the reference trajectory while respecting strict curvature and dynamic constraints, we devise novel cost and heuristic functions in the planner and incorporate linearized warm-starting in the MPC for improved convergence.
elements.sent-2411-426: Furthermore, we propose an online replanning strategy as an extension that enables real-time avoidance of unforeseen obstacles along with pruning techniques that enhances computational efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-427: The proposed framework enables the generation of smooth, obstacle-free, and dynamically feasible trajectories for agricultural vehicles, supporting both offline smoothing and online planning, as demonstrated in experiments on real-world farm datasets.
elements.sent-2411-428: Compared to baseline methods, our approach achieves superior performance in path adherence, safety, computational efficiency, and real-time responsiveness.
elements.sent-2411-429: Future work includes extending the framework to full-field path planning for maximizing field coverage while minimizing deviation, and exploring multi-vehicle collaboration strategies to optimally allocate agricultural tasks while maintaining global constraint satisfaction.. \bibliographystyle{ieeetr.}
